question_title: Will the Joint Commission (Comissão Mista) on Brazil's MP 1.357/2026 approve its report (parecer) before May 31, 2026?
summary: The Joint Commission for MP 1.357/2026 has not even been installed as of May 21, 2026, with only ~10 days remaining before the May 31 deadline. Historical data from 2023-2024 shows zero MPs received a Comissão Mista parecer within 21 days of publication — the fastest was 41 days, and the median is 78-91 days. The commission still needs to be installed, elect a president, designate a relator, have that relator analyze 112 filed amendments, draft a parecer, and hold a committee vote — all before May 31. There is no institutional urgency pressure (trancamento de pauta begins June 26). While this is a politically salient MP, political priority has historically accelerated timelines from 80+ days to ~41 days at best, not to 19 days. A 3% probability accounts for the theoretical possibility of unprecedented political fast-tracking while reflecting the overwhelming procedural and historical evidence against YES.

factors.description: Zero historical precedent: No MP in 2023-2024 received a Comissão Mista parecer within 21 days of publication. The fastest was 41 days — nearly double the available window here (19 days from publication to deadline).

Commission not yet installed: As of May 21, the official tramitation page shows status 'AGUARDANDO INSTALAÇÃO DA COMISSÃO' with no relator named and 112 amendments to analyze — all procedural steps (installation, president election, relator designation, amendment analysis, parecer drafting, committee vote) must complete in ~10 days.
No urgency regime pressure: Trancamento de pauta doesn't begin until June 26, 2026 — there is no institutional mechanism forcing Congress to prioritize this MP's commission process before May 31.

Political salience as government priority: The taxa das blusinhas MP was signed with full political fanfare by Lula, affecting millions of consumers and major platforms. Government-priority MPs historically move faster than average.
High amendment count (112 filed): Creates substantial analytical work for the relator, making rapid parecer preparation more difficult than for a low-controversy MP.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% (NO is overwhelmingly likely)**

**Key reasoning:**

1. **Zero historical precedent**: In the 2023-2024 dataset of ~24 Brazilian MPs, not a single one received a Comissão Mista parecer within 21 days. The fastest was 41 days — more than double the available window.

2. **Commission not even installed yet**: As of today (May 21), the official page shows "AGUARDANDO INSTALAÇÃO DA COMISSÃO." No relator named, no work begun. Multiple procedural steps (installation → president election → relator designation → analysis of 112 amendments → parecer drafting → committee vote) must all complete in ~10 days.

3. **No institutional pressure**: The urgency regime (trancamento de pauta) doesn't kick in until June 26, so there's no mechanism forcing Congress to rush this before May 31.

4. **Mild offset for political salience**: This is a government-priority MP, but even high-priority MPs have only been observed to accelerate from ~85 days to ~41 days — nowhere near the 19-day window required here.

The 3% allows for theoretically possible but historically unprecedented political intervention to fast-track all procedural steps within days.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the Joint Commission (Comissão Mista) on Brazil's MP 1.357/2026 approve its report (parecer) before May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43559)

[Congresso busca compensar varejo nacional e atenuar efeitos do fim da 'taxa das blusinhas' - Diário do Grande ABC](https://www.dgabc.com.br/Noticia/4320143/congresso-busca-compensar-varejo-nacional-e-atenuar-efeitos-do-fim-da-taxa-das-blusinhas-)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, before May 31, 2026 (Brasília time), the Joint Commission (Comissão Mista) designated to analyze Medida Provisória 1.357/2026 has formally approved a parecer (opinion/report) on the MP, as reflected on the official tramitation page at https://www.congressonacional.leg.br/materias/medidas-provisorias/-/mpv/174123 (for example, the page records 'Parecer aprovado pela Comissão Mista' or an equivalent registered approval of a commission opinion). A commission parecer that recommends rejection still counts as a YES, as the parecer itself was approved. Mere designation of a relator, presentation of a draft parecer that has not been voted on, or plenary votes that bypass the commission do NOT count. Otherwise the question resolves NO.
fine_print: If the official Congresso Nacional tramitation page is unavailable, the resolver may use the Câmara dos Deputados or Senado Federal official tramitation pages for MPV 1357/2026, or reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). If MP 1.357/2026 is withdrawn, rejected by either plenary, or otherwise terminated before a Joint Commission parecer is approved, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Zero historical precedent: No MP in 2023-2024 received a Comissão Mista parecer within 21 days of publication. The fastest was 41 days.
Commission not yet installed: As of May 21, the commission is still 'awaiting installation' with no relator named, and 112 amendments to analyze.
No urgency regime pressure: Trancamento de pauta doesn't begin until June 26, removing the institutional incentive to rush.
Political salience: This is a government priority MP (taxa das blusinhas) which could theoretically accelerate proceedings.
High amendment count (112): Creates significant analytical work for the relator, making fast turnaround more difficult.

input.anchor: Historical base rate: 0% - Zero out of ~24 observed MPs in 2023-2024 received a Comissão Mista parecer within 21 days of publication
input.assessment: The evidence overwhelmingly points to NO. The commission hasn't even been installed yet as of today (May 21), with only ~10 days until the May 31 deadline. Historical data shows no MP has ever received a parecer within 21 days, let alone within 19 days (May 12 to May 31). The fastest recorded case was 41 days. There are 112 amendments to analyze, no relator named, and no institutional pressure (urgency regime starts June 26). While this is a politically salient MP, political priority has historically sped things up from 80+ days to ~41 days at best - not to 19 days. The probability should be very low, around 2-3%.
input.calibration_notes: Base rate is essentially 0%. Even with political salience adjustment, the procedural steps remaining (installation, relator designation, amendment analysis, parecer drafting, committee vote) make this timeline nearly impossible. Setting at 3% to account for unknown unknowns and the theoretical possibility of extraordinary political intervention.
input.key_uncertainties: Could there be an unprecedented political intervention to fast-track this? Could the government pressure Congress to install the commission, name a relator, and vote on a parecer all within 10 days? This would be historically unprecedented but not technically impossible.
input.tool_audit: Metaculus question fetch: successful, confirmed no CP available. Research tool: first query returned excellent empirical data on MP timelines; second query failed to access live sources. Direct URL fetch of official tramitation page: successful, confirmed current status. Web search: returned relevant news articles confirming current status and political dynamics.
input.update_triggers: 1. Commission installed and relator named within 1-2 days (would slightly increase probability). 2. News reports of government pushing for rapid commission action before May 31. 3. Scheduled commission meeting announced for before May 31.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was very effective for the first query, providing empirical data that strongly anchors the forecast. The second research query couldn't access live sources, but direct fetch_url filled that gap. The combination of historical base rates and current status data makes this a high-confidence forecast. The main friction was the research sub-agent's inability to access Brazilian government websites, but the direct fetch tool worked well as a fallback.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**All factors verified against the research trace.**

**Factor 1 (zero historical precedent, logit=-3.0):** The research tool returned a detailed table of 9 MPs from 2023-2024 that received parecers, with the fastest being MP 1238/2024 at 41 days. The claim "zero out of ~24 observed MPs" is supported: 9 received parecers + 15 expired without one = 24. Fully grounded.

**Factor 2 (commission not yet installed, logit=-2.0):** The fetch_url of the official tramitation page (congressonacional.leg.br/materias/medidas-provisorias/-/mpv/174123) returned "AGUARDANDO INSTALAÇÃO DA COMISSÃO" and confirmed 112 amendments submitted. The claim that no relator has been named is explicitly confirmed. Fully grounded.

**Factor 3 (no urgency regime pressure, logit=-1.0):** Both the official page fetch and the question background confirm the urgency regime begins June 26, 2026. Fully grounded.

**Factor 4 (political salience, logit=+0.5):** News articles from multiple outlets (Diário do Grande ABC, InfoMoney, Correio do Estado) confirm this is a high-priority government measure. The modest positive logit and low confidence (0.5) are appropriate. Fully grounded.

**Factor 5 (high amendment count, logit=-0.5):** The official page confirms 112 amendments. Fully grounded.

**Double-counting check:** No issues. Factor 1 (historical impossibility) and Factor 2 (current status) draw on different evidence — one is base rate data from the Câmara API, the other is the live status page. Factors 3 and 5 are also independent.

**Wrong-direction check:** All signs are correct — negative factors have negative logits, the positive factor has a positive logit.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** The agent directly fetched the official tramitation page specified in the resolution criteria and verified the current status. The agent correctly understands that only a formally approved parecer (not relator designation or draft parecer) counts for YES.

**Counterargument quality:** Strong. The agent constructs a coherent alternative scenario involving behind-the-scenes deals, pre-prepared parecers, and marathon sessions. It identifies four specific things that would shift the estimate. This is a genuine adversarial exercise.

**Probability assessment:** The 3% estimate is consistent with the evidence. The anchor (0% base rate from ~24 MPs) combined with strong negative factors (commission not installed, 10 days remaining, 112 amendments, no urgency) supports a very low probability. The 3% allows for unprecedented political fast-tracking, which is appropriate — zero historical cases in a limited sample (24 MPs, 2023-2024 only) doesn't mean literally impossible. I independently estimate ~2-4%, consistent with the agent's 3%.

**Confidence assessment:** 0.88 is appropriate given the strong empirical data, official page confirmation, and multiple corroborating sources.

**Tool audit check:** The agent claims 0 tool failures, consistent with the ground truth (0 errors in 7 calls). The agent notes the second research query "failed to access live sources" — this is slightly imprecise (the research call succeeded but the sub-agent within it couldn't fetch live URLs), but not materially misleading since the agent compensated by doing direct fetch_url and web_search calls.

No errors found.
